speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, interview with Felix Pearson, August 12, 1996. We're still in Lask. And I wanted to ask you about your father's mother, if you would describe her. First, her full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her full name was Feigan Naches. I think the last name was [INAUDIBLE], because I think it was she had married-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it was [INAUDIBLE]. You know, it never came up when I was a child what the last name was. Was Feigan Naches. Everyone in the city, in the town knew Feigan Naches.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do for a  living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was selling fruits and vegetables on the market. And I remember wintertime, when it was cold, and my father-- you know, we were tailors-- he was a tailor. He used to send out a worker with a pot with hot coal, you know, those tailor-- that you put in an iron. And she would put it underneath her big skirt and that would keep her warm. But she was a pious, learned, decent woman, proud woman. She was tall in posture, and she carried herself that way. In the synagogue, she was what was called a [NON-ENGLISH]. She prayed in the large synagogue. And a woman with-- at that time, many, many women couldn't read Hebrew. So there was this woman that would read and they would repeat, and she was-- she did that. And she, on Saturday, she would sit all day over-- over the Seder, and mostly in the afternoon, but the [NON-ENGLISH], and read it aloud to whoever passed by could hear. And across from where she lived, there was this movie house, and they opened the movie house at 4:30. And Jewish people started to go to the movie house. And there were some people that were spiteful. Most Jews wouldn't do that, but there were people that didn't believe in God, that were communist. That was very modern at the time. They wanted just to be spiteful. And my grandmother would get very upset, very upset, and she would let them know. I know she didn't, you know-- but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was a strong-minded person. I remember we used to come to visit her. She would give us fruits, cut up pieces of apple, but it was with love. I only later realized when I began to understand under what circumstances she lived. My father would always try to help her, and he did help, of course. He did help. He helped the entire family, because he came from surroundings that were worse off than he was when he-- we had this tailor shop and we lived comfortably. So he helped his family, and he wouldn't do a thing with that. Talking to his mother, she was a wise woman. But I'm sorry to say that one of the advices wasn't so good, because her mother's-- I mean, my mother's-- from my mother's family that lived in Argentina, they were very well off, had sent what was called a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, papers to go to Argentina for my father when I was one year old-- one years old. And my mother said, you go ahead, and I'll come later. But my father went for advice to his mother, and she said, you're going to go to a far country. You'll become a goy. You'll have to work for someone else. Here, you have your own factory. You're going away from the family. So my father listened to his mother, not to his wife, to the regret of the entire family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the war, I was in contact with this-- with the family in Argentina, and they all reminded me. Says-- they said that everyone could have been saved. But I don't know how everyone would have been, but at least a large part of the family. But again, was [INAUDIBLE]. What can you do? Did people leave the-- Lask, that you knew, before the Germans came? Did you feel that people were packing up and leaving, to Israel, to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, people went to Palestine. We have cousins that came to our town. And my father helped them. I remember seeing him giving them some money. They went to [? Haksharat, ?] [? Haksharat. ?] They went to Israel in-- had to be '36, because I was very young at the time. And as a matter of fact, two or three cousins of-- my cousins, because they were my father's brother's children. And so they did go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: But people didn't believe those stories that you read in the paper. I remember distinctly my father reading the paper about Jewish families having to purchase the ashes of a cremated person. We're talking now-- we're talking now in the '30s. That could have been '38 or '37. My father said that is propaganda, because the Germans were in Poland in our hometown in the First World War. And when they saw an older person carry a sack of coal or potatoes, they would pick it up and bring it to the house. He said, how is it possible? It's the same people. And what really stopped immigration from-- and I'm talking during the war years, during the war years. You ask me from before the war. There wasn't much immigration from before the war. Here and there, somebody would go to America, to Palestine. But people lived their way and they were satisfied, in a way. You know, like there's a Jewish saying, when the worm lives in the [NON-ENGLISH]. What is [NON-ENGLISH] in English? Near the cold-- no. Near the heat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What was it? Can't think of the word. Anyway, it's a-- it's a fruit-- vegetable that grows in the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the-- it's the radish? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's bigger, much bigger. Anyway-- anyway, so he's satisfied, right? And these people were satisfied the way they lived. It's OK, you'll think of it later. There was-- there-- people were satisfied.

speaker: interviewee
text: But during the war years, some people ran to the eastern part of Poland, where Russia had occupied. And they came back filthy, swollen, hungry. They said-- people said, why you run any place? Let's say here. No one really believed that this can happen. Who in their mind could believe that such things would-- would wind up the way-- the way it did? What happened as the Germans came into your town? Do you remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, like I said, the day before, on Thursday, the 31st of August, was the sign about mobilization, and we were so happy. Friday morning, there were airplanes flying over our town. There was someone-- I don't know if it was a soldier or a civilian-- killed on one of the bridges. There were two small rivers going through our town, and someone was killed on the bridge. Everyone, you know, was secure that-- people weren't being killed in our town normally, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't remember about Saturday. But Sunday, planes were flying over our-- our town. And the Polish policemen-- you know, we were hiding in the-- in the corn field right near the houses, because the field was right outside the town. And with that rifle, he was shooting to the airplane. Those airplanes came down and started to throw, you know, bombs. And a lot of people-- there were 39 people that were injured from shrapnel. I don't know if there were any killed. I remember standing at a pump and pumping the water, where my father was treating the wounded. It was a very primitive way. It was very disorganized. No one really was prepared for such thing, you know? And as a  matter of fact, one of the shrapnels had hit a friend-- my friend's mother. But she never went out of bed since then, you know? And that was-- that was the beginning. It was step by step by step. Let's go from the steps. What was the next step?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we're out-- well, they started to come to the houses and takes things. At first, they asked if they can take it. And you know, we had officers that came to our house that my father should sell for them leather coats. And we did. But they would come around into the house, and they saw-- I remember distinctly, we had a picture of Adam and Eve. It was in my parents' bedroom. So they asked if they can take it. [LAUGHS] You couldn't say no, because we knew that they were going just in houses and taking it. But if they asked, of course, you said yes. So my father signed on a paper that it's a gift.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, things started to get rough for-- the very first, they came in in '39. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the very first Independence Day, which was 11th of November, they took the intelligentsia and priests and the rabbi, you know, to sweep the streets right there. They started-- this was the beginning of degradation. This was the beginning of undermining our spirits, our self-respect, you know? This is how gradually the thing grew, where everyone was shocked because we respected these people and-- so they had to sweep the streets. Then it started by just grabbing people in the street to go to work, to clean the toilets, to-- to all kind of dirty, kind of insulting, humiliating work. Any pogroms by the Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we didn't have anything. They were afraid, too. But after a while when things got settled, there were people, Polish people, that would report on Jewish people. This one is rich and this one is that. Like they called my father into the-- the gendarmerie, and they wanted-- we had some fabrics that we had put underground for after the war. And someone must have told them that my father was a custom tailor and we had fabric. So they beat him up. They cut his back. You know, you see, sometimes those-- I just saw the other day where they beat this sailor with a whip, and he used the term that they cut his back into slices. And my father came back from that beating, we had-- well, you probably never heard this expression, a [NON-ENGLISH], which is not a doctor. He is like today what could say a paramedic. But this man knew everything. And it so happens he is on the picture that-- with my father. It was also on that [INAUDIBLE]. And he came to the house and he did whatever he could. But for a long time, my father was laying on his back-- on his stomach, couldn't lay on the back. And a lot of people felt hurt because my father was respected in town. And that's what they did. They picked on individuals where it affected, psychologically, everyone in town. They took the most respected citizen-- citizens in town and they humiliated them. That was gradually, gradually too, you know. And it worked. It worked to try to-- to keep up to this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: First, they took the synagogue and they made it into a stable. They they took the beth midrash. You asked me about synagogues. I forgot, but it was a beth midrash where people were sitting and learning day and night, day and night. Synagogue, you know, was a more-- wouldn't use the word elegant, but it wasn't used for those purposes. In a beth midrash, people would sit-- it was a forum-- and argue and learn. From this they made-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what is it called? A milk-- a dairy farm, you know, where you put cows in and they milked. And they produced the milk for-- everything for the German population.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the fact is that they didn't realize that there were so many [NON-ENGLISH] in our town. A lady that lived next door where we had our shop, every morning when she would see me when I was a child, she would pat me on the head, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, [INAUDIBLE]. My Polish nickname was [NON-ENGLISH]. She would give me a candy. And I would say thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in Polish language, Miss-- you don't say Miss, but at the end of the name is-- like her name was Green, it was Greenovna. Greenovna is a Polish name. All of a sudden, she said to me, don't call me Greenovna, my name is Green, you know? And there were many like-- many people like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but in a way, we lived with them in a relative-- I wouldn't say peace, but it was an accustomed way. We knew how the relations that we had with them, but everything changed. First, then they took away our radios, whoever had a radio. And they came to the houses and took what they wanted. And then in 1940, we were all transferred into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they place the ghetto in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town. They took part of the older part, you know? It was guarded by Jewish police, and-- which some of them weren't better than the Germans, you know? But they put fear in our hearts in different ways. Like, we organized-- there was an organized, or appointed rather, leadership of the ghetto, the [NON-ENGLISH], or Judenrat. No, I think they called them Judenrat, where a man by the name of Cochran was the leader. And this-- this committee had to prepare people to go to work, to send to work to the-- to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when people came back to the ghetto they would bring-- some would bring some potatoes, some bread, or some coal. So they wanted to stop that. So they found on two Jewish boys, that happened to be immigrants from another town, you know? From this town, it would have been too much. So they-- they picked those two Jewish boys. They found on them potatoes. They took the entire town out on a field outside of town to-- you have to watch the way these two boys were hung.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, at that time, I was 14 years old. I mean, we were brought up not like today's young people, knowledgeable about that. We were protected. Our parents were very protective. We didn't see any hangings. And all this, you know, was another humiliation, another put a scare in not to do this, not to-- to behave. But somehow, we did everything with a deep belief that we will survive. We were hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you live in the ghetto? What kind of rooms were you given?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandfather-- grandfather, the house that he had, we-- we had several rooms in our house. But here, we moved everything into one room, where we had our bed, our kitchen. It was my father's worktable. And that's where we eat-- we ate. So it was all in one room, and no one complained.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the food ran out, and we were kosher, I mean, that was obvious. It wasn't like-- yes, people were kosher. But we had to live, so my mother started cooking horse meat, and we liked it. My mother made it like baked goods. It was delicious. So we figured they can't defeat us, right? We are together. They opened, at that time, a factory in the city. As a matter of fact, the building where we lived, they made a large factory for German civilian clothes. So I started to go to that factory to work in order to get the rations for food. [SIGHS] And my mother was so proud, you know? Her son is working, and so we will be OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was going on like that, but the repression was getting worse and worse, because especially, when the war broke out and they attacked Russia. So they raided the houses for overcoats, any furs. And everyone in town knew who had a fur. And you had-- and there were people that reported. So rather than wait and be beaten up, people delivered. It's strange to picture this, but people delivered their possessions to the-- to the-- that office, wherever it was decided to.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, let me just go back a bit when we first moved into the ghetto. My father was in contact with the peasants that were his customers. So they would, like, wanted a suit turned from one side on the other, because there was no fabric. So it was worn out on the sides or was turned out on there. So we were sitting in the attic and sewing it, because they took away all our machines. And there was in that Judenrat, in that building, they had some machines that you could rent, but for an hour or two, what you needed for long seams, you know? And they would bring some food for that worker to eat it. But that lasted a short time, and they started to send people to work camps. There were no-- we didn't know about concentration camps, to Poznan and difference, [INAUDIBLE], I remember. But I was still too young for that. And they were people that didn't-- didn't have this job, these jobs that the Germans needed. So these people were-- constantly were sent out to work, you know? And of course, after awhile, we heard that other cities, other towns are being deported to the ghetto Lodz where we were, you know? I remember sitting on the second floor, and we heard these trucks rumble through. And I looked out and I saw people standing on the trucks, and these were people from the city of [? Pabianice ?] taken to [? Chelmno ?] you know? No, these are people from [POLISH] that were taken to Lodz. Because we didn't see those that were taken to [? Chelmno. ?] These were taken to the city of Lodz to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it didn't take too long. You know, at that time, everything, you know-- every day was like a year. But looking at it now, this-- these transports we saw in May, and in August, they came to deport our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: August of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, of course, it's August of '42. I'm too far already. It's '42. And it was so sad. We knew. We heard. We prepared. What did we prepare? My-- my father made me sort of a blouse with a zipper, you know, that I should be able to wear a suit underneath and hide it, and on top, a navy blue blouse. [SIGHS] For my brother, my father made a suit from the fabric that we had, which wasn't for children. It was a very expensive fabric. And on Saturday, I-- you know we-- he wore the suit and we went walking within the ghetto. So it was so small that we went to visit a man that used to work for my father, and he had a garden. And like I said, my-- my younger brother, he was a lively boy and he didn't go through the gate, outside, but he had to climb the-- the fence. And he tore his jacket in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the reason I'm telling you this story-- there is a reason why I'm telling you this story. So of course, this man that worked for my father, he fixed that jacket. Later on, in the ghetto, when I knew my brother, you know, was already, you know, taken with my mother, and I was assistant foreman in the juvenile part of the factory. A young man came into that with that jacket to work, and I recognized it on him. And I said, you see-- you see the back where this is-- you know? And all I did is ask him not to wear it. And he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were preparing-- preparing. I mean, my parents must have known more than what we were told. And then on-- on August 22, I think it was, because on the 24th we came to the-- to Lodz. Either the-- between the 22nd-- I don't remember the exact date. The town was surrounded, and of course, Judenrats, they came to house-- to the house where we lived, in our grandfather's house. I had a two-year-old cousin that was-- had an appendix operation. And he was laying in the lounge chair in the alley between the houses the way it was in Europe. They didn't bother to pick him up. They shot him right on the spot. And all-- there were 65 people-- 65 people that we were-- that-- from that-- we were all pushed down that main street in the ghetto, which was [POLISH]. And in Jewish, we called it a tannery street because there were tanneries on that street. And they started pushing us and rushing us all into the church. And there was a distance. And whoever couldn't walk, they would shoot on the spot. They shot my grandmother, my father's mother. I don't know, she couldn't-- I'm sure she could walk. She was a strong woman. She was an old woman, but she could-- it must have been because I didn't see her. I didn't see her. Then my father's cousin-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father's uncle was a respected man in town. He was dealing with fish and he was dealing with iron. You know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were collecting pieces of iron, and he would buy it up from them. But he was a respected man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What as his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Moshe ?] [? Falec ?] [? Klein. ?] And he walked with his son. I think it was his youngest son, second to the youngest. And this gendarme came over to him-- this SS man came over to him with a gun. They all held guns in their hands. And he said, if you're not going to move faster, I'm going to shoot you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- his son ran in front of the father and said, you can shoot me. You're not going to shoot my father. So he shot him and he shot his father. And they shot a total of 65 people. There was a common grave in our cemetery. I was in the-- in the hometown-- I mean, my hometown, Lask a number of times. I couldn't find this common grave anymore because the Poles have taken the tombstones and they're building houses out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they chased us into the church [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they didn't let us go in through the main gate, where you open those big two gates. We went in through a small door. And they were standing on the outside and inside and beating us with the-- with the butts of the rifles, with whips. Every single person was beaten getting in. [SIGHS] On that chase, where we were chased into the church, there was one pregnant woman that they bayoneted her in the stomach. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, when we got all into the church-- now, I don't really know the number that we were, but the church was filled up. No hygienic facilities for so many people. So you did whatever you had to do right where you were. And yet, we prayed three times a day. But most what intrigued me was the rabbi gave [NON-ENGLISH]. He married two people in the-- in the church. And I asked my father, how can this be in a church? Because where I grew up, a church was [NON-ENGLISH] because we passed that church, we know all the evil comes out from there because evil, bad things were taught to people in that church against the Jewish people. So we used to turn the head to the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we were in there. And my father said that the rabbi says that any place where there is a minion of Jews, it becomes a holy place. And we were there for three days. I don't remember, two nights or three nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take you on trains to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, I am not finished with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, OK, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after, on the third day, we were all taken out in the square in the front with the big gates, in groups, and were separated, right and left. And I remember being with-- with my father. And three days my father didn't shave, and he had sort of a gray. My father become-- became gray when he was in Pilsudski's army, when he fought for the independence of Poland in 1920. At that time he was 24 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was scared. He looked to me like an old man. I had this-- I don't know, it's all godsend. I thought of the picture that he had in his pocket that he just came from a spa in-- in Poland in Muszyna that's near Krynica. And I pulled out this-- this was like a passport with a picture. And I put it in front of this man and I said schneidermeister. We're going to continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
